checks.a_analysis: The output JSON consists of two main sections corresponding to the two pages of the source PDF. The headings ('Goal 1', 'Goal 2') and the content within the tables (items 1.1 through 2.4) accurately reflect the text found in the source document. No fabricated body text or invented sections were identified. The metadata title is an acceptable AI-generated/filename-based field.

The source document does not contain phone numbers, email addresses, or specific URLs, and neither does the output. The reference to 'NC OneMap Geospatial Portal' is preserved from the source without any added fabricated links or numbers.
The output contains all sections and goals present in the source. Goal 1 and its items (1.1-1.7) from page 1 and Goal 2 and its items (2.1-2.4) from page 2 are all present in the structured JSON.

The source document contains two main table structures. Goal 1 has 7 items, represented in the JSON as 15 rows (7 header rows plus 8 data rows, as item 1.7 contains two distinct data sub-sections). Goal 2 has 4 items, represented in the JSON as 8 rows (4 header rows plus 4 data rows). The row counts in the output perfectly match the logical structure of the source PDF.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: A line-by-line comparison confirms that the full text of each goal and item description is preserved. For instance, item 2.2 in the header correctly includes the long list '...educational institutions, metropolitan planning organizations, and rural planning organizations' and item 1.7 correctly includes both 'Geospatial Services' and 'Other applications' data rows.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: No meaningful text differences were found. OCR-related normalizations (such as replacing non-standard dashes with standard hyphens in terms like 'local-to-state') are within the bounds of acceptable formatting and do not alter the information.

checks.a_analysis: The output does not contain any duplicated text from the source document. Each section and table entry appears exactly once, following the original flow.

The heading hierarchy is inconsistent. 'Goal 1' is correctly identified as a heading (Level 2), but 'Goal 2', which is of equal structural weight in the source document, is rendered as a 'paragraph'. This breaks the logical hierarchy and makes navigation difficult for screen reader users.
checks.b_evidence_source: Goal 2. Collaborate and conduct outreach for more integration of geospatial data in information technology for expanded benefits in the geospatial community in North Carolina.

checks.a_analysis: The table structure for Goal 1 and Goal 2 is corrupted. The output combines multiple visual sections (1.1, 1.2, etc.) into single large tables. Within these tables, the 'header' rows (e.g., containing the text for 1.1) are defined with only one cell and a 'colspan' of 1. However, the subsequent data rows in the same table have two cells. This creates an uneven column count per row without proper spanning, resulting in a structurally invalid table that will not align correctly in a screen reader or renderer.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source shows a header bar (e.g., '1.1. Promote free...') spanning the width of the two-column data section below it.
checks.b_evidence_output: Row 1 of the table has 1 cell with "colspan": 1, while Row 2 has 2 cells.

checks.a_analysis: The reading order of the document is preserved. It follows the title, Goal 1, its sub-sections (1.1-1.7), then Goal 2 and its sub-sections (2.1-2.4) correctly from top to bottom.
There are no standard bulleted or numbered lists in the source document; the numbering is part of the section headers/tables, which the output attempts to represent.
There are no links in the source document, and none were incorrectly added to the output.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML document faithfully replicates the visual organization of the source PDF. The source presents goals and sub-goals in distinct colored blocks/tables. The HTML output groups these sub-goals into a single table under each main Goal heading. This is a logical and acceptable structural adaptation for a responsive HTML layout that preserves the original information flow and hierarchical relationship. A user can easily find the same information in the same relative positions.

The tables in the HTML output are well-rendered. They include clear borders that distinguish rows and columns, and the internal alignment matches the source PDF. For example, section 1.7 correctly maps 'Geospatial Services' and 'Other applications' to their respective multiple points in the right-hand column, just as shown in the source. Headers for sub-goals (e.g., 1.1, 1.2) are clearly identifiable within the table structure.
The source PDF does not contain any meaningful images, logos, or charts (it consists entirely of text and styled table containers). Therefore, no images are missing from the HTML output.

There is no significant duplication of body content. The text for sub-goals 1.1 and 2.1 appears twice: once as a table caption/heading and once as the first row within the table. This is a standard accessible design pattern to provide a name for the table and does not constitute a defect. No paragraphs, data rows, or tables are repeated incorrectly.
